feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

paragraphs.text: FEBEA FEDERATION OF BEAUTY COMPANIES
Paris, 24 October 2025
Subject-matter: Consultation on EU legislation on digital fairness.
Madam, Sir,

FEBEA brings together and represents more than 300 manufacturing companies, more than 80% of which are SMEs, selling cosmetics in France and internationally within the meaning of Regulation (EC) No 1223/2009 (perfumes, make-up, hairdressing products, care products and hygiene and toiletry products), which are marketed through numerous distribution systems. FEBEA thus represents the interests of French companies vis-à-vis national authorities and European authorities through Cosmetics Europe (European Federation of Cosmetics Industries) of which it is a member.

We welcome this consultation work, which aims to gather stakeholders' views on how to ensure fairness for consumers and businesses in the Digital Single Market, improve legal certainty, ensure effective enforcement and prevent market fragmentation. Europe's digital economy is growing rapidly, driven by the digitalisation of trade, the rise of e-commerce and online platforms. This growth affects all sectors, in particular everyday consumer goods and cosmetics.

The European Commission's work on the Digital Fairness Fitness Check of October 2024 assessed the relevance of the consumer protection legal framework in this ever-changing environment. It is now a question of adapting European rules to new digital practices (deceptive patterns, influencers, artistic intelligence, social networks, platforms, etc.) while ensuring fair competition and better consumer trust. This dynamic must take place in a context of simplification and preservation of competitiveness for the benefit of European players, in a highly disturbed international trade environment. In addition to this, and in addition to more traditional counterfeits, 'dupes' have become more popular in our industry in recent years. Products that are illegal and violate many intangible rights, in particular, and to which platforms and other social networks unfortunately facilitate access.
Maintaining the competitiveness of businesses based in Europe

Digital trust is precisely a lever for growth and a key factor in competitiveness for European businesses. The concept of digital fairness must therefore be aimed at protecting consumers from phenomena as diverse as dark patterns, unfair commercial practices by certain influencers, addictive design of digital products and exploiting the vulnerability of certain individuals.
IF ...D
D, D dd,

The explosion in sales of illicit products or illegal methods of resale, which are often carried out by operators outside European territory, should not be ignored! We are particularly mindful of infringing products, 'dupes', non-compliant and/or dangerous products, resale outside authorised networks and more generally the various forms of unfair competition and free-riding. It must be noted that the active role of certain influencers and other dark patterns plays a key role in the explosion of these

I See in particular: https://france.representation.ec.europa.eu/informations/la-commission-demande-des-informations-shein-sur-productss-illegaux-et-son-systeme-de-2025-02-06_en Also: https://www.economie.gouv.fr/files/files/directions_services/dgccrf/media-document/cp-dgccrf-SHEIN-sanctionne-40millions.pdf Or, to cite another example: https://www.cnil.fr/fr/cookies-deposes-sans-consentement-la-cnil-sanctionne-shein-dune-amende-de-150-millions-dEUR
FEBEA: 137, rue de l'Université 75007 Paris – Tel: +33 (0)1 56 69 67 89 e-mail: febea@febea.fr – website: www.febea.fr
phenomena, which threatens consumer safety, French and European industrial know-how, the reputation of brands and fair competition within the internal market.

By way of illustration, by taking advantage of infringing alternatives, the 'dupes' phenomenon mentioned above standardises the consumption of low-quality and dangerous products, exposing vulnerable consumers to risks while harming legitimate European businesses. Tackling this trend at EU level is therefore essential to defend consumers and enhance the value of European brands.
**A European framework to be consolidated**

The willingness to address the issue of influencers at European level can only be welcomed. National initiatives tend to multiply, in various forms, and European harmonisation remains the top priority on this and the others mentioned above. In addition, work on existing texts should be prioritised rather than creating new legislative vehicles. A robust framework exists, although it deserves adjustments in line with evolving market practices, especially from non-European operators. The Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (2005/29/EC), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Digital Services Act (DSA), but also the Digital Markets Act (DMA) and the AI Act (Regulation (EC) No 2024/1689).

There is also a need to strengthen controls and cooperation between national authorities, to ensure uniform interpretation of texts at European level, but also to clarify responsibilities between sellers, distributors and online intermediaries in all their diversity (platforms, influencers, social networks, etc.).
For a regulated sector such as the cosmetics industry, this implies that products sold online strictly comply with the same safety and traceability requirements as those sold at physical outlets.
**Supporting a competitive, transparent and fair Digital Single Market**

Europe already has a robust regulatory framework to protect consumers and ensure fair digital practices. The challenge is to adapt it, but also to better implement it, to strengthen cooperation between Member States and to better support businesses. In order to support a harmonised, pragmatic and effective implementation approach, it would therefore be appropriate to create a dedicated European coordination body on digital fairness to harmonise practices and ensure implementation.
uniform regulations. In particular, to better coordinate the actions that would be required in the face of very large players located outside our borders.

The development of sector-specific guidelines and codes of conduct will help companies to identify and characterise unfair practices as one of the other ways of working. In particular in the activation of notices and take down requests.

The "Know Your Business Customer (KYBC)" principle should now be extended more broadly. The obligation to verify the identity and legitimacy of commercial customers should indeed be extended to all online intermediaries and not limited to marketplaces. Many platforms lack sufficient verification procedures, which complicates the trap and responsibility of actors involved in illegal activities, including the sale of infringing or 'dup' products. Like physical points of sale, all intermediaries should be able to identify their business partners in order to ensure the safety and compliance of the products sold, and to ensure effective consumer redress.

Furthermore, the ongoing work to remove the customs thresholds of EUR 150 needs to be continued and strengthened with a subsidiarity principle making platforms liable as presumed importers in the presence of non-traceable sellers. Also in the context of developments in the DSA, trademarks
FEBEA: 137, rue de l'Université 75007 Paris - Tel: +33 (0)1 56 69 67 89 e-mail: febea@febea.fr - website: www.febea.fr
should be recognised astrusted Flaggersto facilitate the removal of illegal content and products.

Finally, unauthorised sales constitute an unfair commercial practice that harms the value of trademarks, deceives consumers and indirectly facilitates the circulation of infringing or non-compliant products (which are, in memory, dangerous for the consumer). These sales, which violate the sealing of selective distribution networks fraudulently, weaken consumer confidence in legitimate brands, harm authorised sellers and severely affect competition. In the digital context, the problem is amplifying due to ease of dissemination through online platforms. It is therefore recommended that these sales be legally qualified as unfair practices and that platforms be held accountable for their prevention, including through the verification of sellers and the swift removal of illegitimate advertisements.
